common_name: Listada de Gandia Eggplant (Striped Italian Heirloom Eggplant)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun after the last frost date; in zone 9 also transplant in August for a fall crop — eggplants often produce their most abundant crop in the cooler fall weather.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Listada de Gandia is one of the most beautiful and visually distinctive eggplant varieties — a traditional Italian heirloom from the Valencia region of Spain producing oval to oblong, medium-large fruits with a stunning two-tone pattern of cream-white base with vivid purple-lavender stripes or splashes covering much of the surface. No two fruits have exactly the same pattern, making each harvest visually unique. Beyond its striking appearance, Listada de Gandia is prized for its exceptionally mild, tender, low-bitterness flesh — the thin skin and low seed content make it one of the finest eggplants for fresh preparations, grilling, and Italian dishes where eggplant bitterness would be unwelcome. The mild, creamy flesh has a slightly sweet, delicate flavor that is quite different from the stronger flavor of large black globe eggplants. Harvest at medium size (4–6 inches long) when the skin is shiny — the stripes fade and seeds become noticeable if left too long. As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds can be saved true-to-type. The ornamental quality of the fruits and foliage makes Listada de Gandia attractive even as an edible ornamental in mixed garden beds and containers. In zone 9, eggplants are among the most heat-tolerant vegetables, thriving in the Gulf South summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (August–October in zone 9) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Shogoin is an excellent dual-purpose turnip — the mild, sweet roots are outstanding for fresh eating and the tender tops are milder-flavored than most American turnip greens.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Shogoin is a Japanese heirloom turnip cultivar that is outstanding in two distinct ways: it produces unusually mild, sweet, tender-fleshed white roots appropriate for fresh eating and light cooking, and simultaneously produces exceptionally mild, tender, palatable greens that are significantly less pungent than the traditional American turnip greens grown for Southern cooking. This dual mildness — both roots and tops — makes Shogoin the turnip of choice for those who find standard American turnips too strongly flavored but want to grow a dual-purpose root vegetable for fall and winter. The pure-white, globe-shaped roots reach 2–3 inches in diameter at ideal harvest size — smaller and more tender than Purple Top White Globe when harvested young. The roots are outstanding raw in salads and slaws, steamed or roasted with light seasoning, and pickled (Shogoin is commonly used for Japanese-style pickled turnips). The tender greens can be used raw in salads when young, stir-fried, or lightly sautéed. In zone 9, Shogoin is a fall and winter crop — sow from August through October for harvest from October through February. The mild flavor makes Shogoin an outstanding variety for introducing turnips to gardeners and cooks unfamiliar with or averse to the pungency of traditional varieties.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade; Catlin's Giant produces significantly larger leaves than standard ajuga cultivars — individual leaves can reach 4–6 inches long, creating a bolder, more dramatic groundcover effect.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade; Catlin's Giant is the large-leaved ajuga cultivar — producing individual leaves 3–6 inches long (two to three times the size of standard ajuga cultivars) in a rich, glossy, mahogany-bronze to purplish-bronze color that creates a dramatically bolder groundcover effect than any other bugleweed selection. The large, rounded, glossy leaves with their deep bronze coloration create a texture and visual weight that reads from a greater distance than fine-leaved types — making it effective as a visible groundcover under trees and in areas where it will be seen from across the garden. In spring, upright spikes of blue-violet flowers rise 6–8 inches above the large bronze foliage — the blue against bronze combination is visually striking and attracts bees and other pollinators. Catlin's Giant spreads by surface stolons to form an expanding mat but is slightly slower to fill in than smaller-leaved cultivars because fewer stolons are produced per unit area. The large leaves provide excellent weed suppression once established, creating dense coverage that blocks light to weed seeds below. In zone 9, ajuga is semi-evergreen to evergreen through mild winters. Catlin's Giant may show heat stress in the peak of Gulf South summer — plant in locations with afternoon shade and consistent moisture to help it through the most intense heat periods.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Aztec Grass develops the most vivid cream striping in partial shade — full sun may bleach the variegation to white and stress the fine-textured foliage in Gulf South summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Aztec Grass is a fine-textured, cream-variegated liriope cultivar — producing very narrow, grass-like leaves with cream to ivory-white longitudinal striping on a green base. The very narrow leaf texture (narrower than standard liriope cultivars) gives plantings a soft, grass-like appearance that differs from the bold strap-leaf texture of Big Blue or Gold Band, creating a finer, more delicate groundcover effect appropriate for intimate garden spaces and detailed plantings. The cream-white variegation on the narrow leaves creates a bright, light-reflective effect in shade conditions — useful for illuminating dark areas under tree canopies or alongside dark hardscape features. Aztec Grass forms compact clumping mounds rather than aggressive spreading, making it appropriate for defined groundcover areas and edging applications. In summer, small lavender to pale purple flower spikes appear above the foliage, followed by black berries. Like all liriopes, Aztec Grass is remarkably adaptable in zone 9 — tolerating heat, drought, shade, and the root competition of mature trees. The fine texture makes it an effective underplanting beneath high-canopied specimens where bolder groundcovers would compete visually. Shear to 3 inches in late winter to remove old foliage before new growth emerges.

common_name: Florida King Peach (Large Low-Chill Yellow Freestone Peach)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile soil in full sun; Florida King requires approximately 450–500 chill hours (hours below 45°F) for adequate dormancy break — it is suited for zone 8–9a where chill hours are sufficient but temperatures are mild enough for low-chill fruit production.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Florida King is one of the premier low-chill peach cultivars for the Gulf South — a large-fruited, yellow-fleshed, freestone peach that produces high-quality fruit with approximately 450–500 chill hours of cold requirement, making it adapted to the winter climate of zone 8–9a where higher-chill northern peach varieties fail to break dormancy properly. The large fruits (2.5–3 inches diameter) have golden-yellow skin with an attractive red blush, yellow-orange flesh, small pit (freestone type), and excellent sweet peach flavor. Florida King ripens in May in zone 9 — one of the earliest major peach harvests, ahead of the intense summer heat that can reduce quality in later-ripening cultivars. Like all peaches, Florida King must be thinned aggressively after set: thin to one fruit every 6–8 inches on each branch to allow the remaining fruits to develop to full size — unthinned trees produce large numbers of small, inferior fruits. Prune annually in late winter (February in zone 9) to maintain tree size at 10–12 feet, promote new wood development on which next season's crop will form, and open the canopy to air circulation that reduces disease pressure. Peach leaf curl and brown rot are the primary disease challenges in the humid Gulf South — preventive dormant sprays with copper fungicide are standard practice.
